hazard.summary: Provider quota reset signal is ignored across the API-response-to-CLI-retry-logic boundary, causing retries before the reset time and a rate-limit retry loop.
signals: Attempt 1 failed: You have exhausted your capacity on this model. Your quota will reset after 43s.. Retrying after 11308ms...
Attempt 2 failed: You have exhausted your capacity on this model. Your quota will reset after 31s.. Retrying after 21819ms...
Attempt 3 failed: You have exhausted your capacity on this model. Your quota will reset after 9s.. Retrying after 32245ms...

constraints: Retry logic must preserve provider-declared rate-limit reset signals across the API-response-to-retry-scheduler boundary.
Retry logic must not schedule quota or rate-limit retries before the provider-declared reset time.
Failure classification must not collapse cooldown-bearing quota exhaustion into generic transient failure.
Retry control must count repeated pre-reset retries against a bounded retry budget.
Fallback or routing logic must receive exhausted-quota signals before retrying the same capacity bucket.
verification: Setup: mock the provider to return HTTP 429 with an error body containing `Your quota will reset after 43s` and configure the client retry policy with a shorter exponential backoff delay. Action: run one request through the CLI retry path. Assertion: the next retry is not scheduled before 43 seconds have elapsed.

Setup: mock sequential 429 responses containing `Your quota will reset after 31s` and `Your quota will reset after 9s`. Action: execute retries under the normal retry scheduler. Assertion: each scheduled retry delay is at least the provider-declared reset interval for that response.

Setup: provide both a generic exponential backoff value and a provider reset interval where the reset interval is longer. Action: trigger a quota-exhaustion retry decision. Assertion: the selected delay equals or exceeds the provider reset time.

Setup: configure a simulated RPM bucket that remains saturated when requests arrive before reset. Action: run the CLI against repeated 429 quota responses. Assertion: the client does not send additional requests before reset and does not enter a continuous retry loop.

Setup: configure fallback routing with one model returning quota exhaustion and another model available. Action: trigger a request that receives the exhausted-model signal. Assertion: the exhausted-model signal is visible to routing or fallback logic and the client does not keep retrying only the exhausted model.
notes: Dominant mechanism: provider reset-after timing is ignored by retry scheduling. Secondary context: the issue also reports a Windows node-pty crash, including `Error: AttachConsole failed`, but that crash is not the primary hazard. routing_impact omitted — canonical mapping unclear, requires human judgment.

constraints: Clients must delay quota retries until at least the provider-declared reset time.
Clients must expose quota exhaustion and reset timing to retry, fallback, and routing decisions.
Clients must cap or suppress retries before a known provider cooldown expires.
anti_patterns: using generic exponential backoff for quota responses with explicit reset times
retrying an exhausted model before its provider-declared cooldown expires
hiding quota-reset metadata inside user-facing error text only
treating provider capacity exhaustion as ordinary transient transport failure
